seo.title: DSPy Guardrails for Recruitment/HR
seo.description: Compliance guide for DSPy agents in recruitment/hr. Meet EEOC guidelines, NYC Local Law 144 requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Recruitment/HR teams using DSPy face heightened compliance risks due to the framework's optimization capabilities, which can inadvertently amplify biases in resume screening and candidate ranking. Optimized DSPy modules may overfit to training data containing historical hiring biases, violating EEOC guidelines and state laws against discrimination. Vex provides essential guardrails by verifying DSPy module outputs against regulatory assertions at inference time, preventing discriminatory decisions before they impact candidates.

complianceRequirements.description: AI hiring tools must not discriminate based on protected characteristics like race, gender, age, or disability
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex verifies DSPy outputs reject decisions correlating protected attributes with negative outcomes, catching bias before candidate notifications

complianceRequirements.description: Requires annual bias audits and candidate notification when AI is used in employment decisions
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex logs all verification failures with audit trails and flags AI usage for disclosure compliance

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates risk management, transparency, and human oversight for recruitment AI systems
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex enforces transparency assertions and blocks high-risk outputs requiring human review

complianceRequirements.description: Prohibits screening tools from discriminating against candidates with disabilities or protected classes
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex detects and blocks reasoning chains that infer disability from resume gaps or phrasing

industryRisks.name: Overfitted Prompt Bias
industryRisks.description: DSPy optimization on biased training data creates screening modules that systematically reject protected groups
industryRisks.example: Module optimized on historical data rejects 80% of resumes with 'parental leave' mentions, violating EEOC

industryRisks.name: Faulty Chain-of-Thought
industryRisks.description: CoT reasoning produces plausible but discriminatory logic paths during candidate evaluation
industryRisks.example: "Recent college grad → likely inexperienced → reject" disproportionately affects younger candidates

industryRisks.name: Uncaught Assertion Failures
industryRisks.description: DSPy modules bypass internal assertions, allowing biased scores to reach hiring decisions
industryRisks.example: Gender inference from name triggers negative scoring despite neutrality assertions

industryRisks.name: Module Composition Drift
industryRisks.description: Composed DSPy pipelines create emergent bias distributions not present in individual modules
industryRisks.example: Resume parser + skills matcher + cultural fit scorer → rejects non-traditional career paths

implementation.title: Wrapping Resume Screener with Vex
implementation.description: Verify DSPy resume screening module prevents protected characteristic discrimination

implementation.title: Candidate Ranking Guardrails
implementation.description: Prevent ranking modules from creating discriminatory score distributions

implementation.title: Interview Question Generator Compliance
implementation.description: Ensure generated interview questions comply with EEOC prohibited inquiry rules

caseStudyScenario.heading: DSPy in Recruitment/HR: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
A mid-sized tech company deployed a DSPy-powered resume screener to handle 10,000 weekly applications. The system used a composed pipeline of resume parser, skills matcher, and cultural fit scorer, optimized on 3 years of historical hiring data. After two weeks, HR noticed qualified candidates from underrepresented groups consistently ranked in the bottom quartile despite strong technical qualifications.

The issue stemmed from module composition drift: the cultural fit scorer's Chain-of-Thought reasoning inferred 'startup experience' as essential, systematically penalizing career changers and diverse candidates with non-traditional paths. EEOC complaint patterns emerged as rejected candidates compared notes on LinkedIn.
caseStudyScenario.outcome:
Vex guardrails wrapping each DSPy module forward() call caught the issue during inference. The diversity assertion failed 87% of rankings, triggering immediate human review. Post-deployment audit revealed the cultural fit module's CoT was generating plausible but discriminatory logic ("lacks fast-paced environment experience → risky hire").

After retraining with Vex-verified examples and adding protected characteristic assertions, the system achieved 92% compliance while maintaining 85% of original precision, avoiding a potential $1.2M EEOC settlement.

cta.heading: Comply with Recruitment/HR Regulations Using Vex
cta.description: Integrate Vex with your DSPy hiring pipeline today to prevent bias violations and ensure regulatory compliance.
